one_line_takeaway: By probing a small, automatically-selected set of attention-head outputs at the last prompt token, AISA extracts a lightweight intent signal that matches or beats commercial jailbreak detectors and, when used to steer decoding logits in a single forward pass, suppresses jailbreak compliance (Strong-Reject <2.0) across 12 LLMs and 13 datasets while leaving utility and false-refusal rates close to the undefended baseline.
key_finding: AISA's attention-head probe signal is competitive with or exceeds strong commercial detectors on mean accuracy across 13 datasets: from Llama3.1-8B-I it reaches 0.9193 mean ACC/COR (vs GPT-4o-mini 0.8909, GPT-4.1-mini 0.8937), and from Llama2-13B-I it reaches 0.9205, exceeding GPT-5-mini's 0.9200. All academic baselines tested (Jailbreak-Classifier 0.8618, NemoGuard 0.7928, GradSafe 0.8324, SPDetector 0.6444, Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-86M 0.5519) score lower. In the end-to-end defense task, AISA keeps Strong-Reject scores below 2.0 across all 7 malicious/adversarial test sets for all 3 unaligned base models tested (e.g. Mistral-7B-U: AISA mean SR ~1.05-1.5 vs vanilla ~2.6-3.5), while producing false-refusal counts close to the undefended vanilla model and far below prompt-modifying baselines ICD and SAGE, which achieve marginally lower SR but at much higher over-refusal cost (e.g. on Llama2-7B-U + XST, SAGE=41 and ICD=13 false refusals vs AISA's much lower reported counts in Fig 9). On 4 utility benchmarks (GSM8K, BoolQ, MMLU, MMLU-Pro), AISA-equipped models show negligible capability degradation (e.g. Llama3.1-8B-I: 82.79->82.79 GSM8K, 71.44->71.44 MMLU). A cosine-similarity analysis of per-head linear-probe weights across Llama3.1-8B alignment variants (instruct, uncensored, dark, safety-aligned) shows widespread similarity above a 3-sigma significance threshold of 0.265, which the authors interpret as evidence that the same underlying 'safety awareness' representation persists across fine-tuning objectives, including malicious ones.
core_contribution: AISA is a training-light, single-forward-pass jailbreak defense for LLMs that (1) localizes an 'intrinsic safety awareness' signal inside the model's own activations via spatiotemporal linear probing, finding the strongest, most stable intent signal in the scaled dot-product outputs of a small, automatically selected subset (top-K, e.g. 16) of attention heads at the final structural token (e.g. the '\n' before generation begins), and (2) uses the resulting scalar 'safety score' q to steer decoding logits at generation time via a two-threshold policy (passive / modulated / active regions) toward a canonical refusal template, without modifying model parameters, adding external modules, or requiring multi-pass inference. The authors also construct a purpose-built training corpus (ALL-4, aggregating EJ-OO, FQ-PH, WJB-s, and JBC, ~8k/3k/3k train/val/test) for training the per-head linear probes, and argue (Fig. 5) that this curated data, not just the probing method, is responsible for much of AISA's detection strength.
threat_model: Prompt-level jailbreak attacks against LLM deployments where the defender cannot (or does not want to) modify base model weights: (1) in-the-wild jailbreak prompts from real users, (2) optimization/search- or gradient-based jailbreak prompts (e.g. GCG-style, AdvBench-derived), and (3) automated red-teaming/LLM-generated jailbreak prompts. The paper explicitly scopes itself to prompt-based attacks reflecting real deployment risk (Sec 2.1); it notes that white-box attacks that directly modify model parameters fall outside its standard threat model, though it reports AISA still defends against them. Defended targets span the alignment spectrum: safety-aligned, standard instruct, uncensored ('maximal helpfulness' fine-tunes), and deliberately malicious 'dark' models, plus models with safety-relevant activation directions ablated (-R variants), across Llama, Mistral, Qwen, and GPT-OSS families (7B-20B, dense and MoE).
limitations: The paper's own ablation (Table 5) shows the threshold/boost configuration materially trades off safety vs. utility -- an over-aggressive setting (SOT=0.15) roughly doubles false refusals (FRC 47 vs 14/23 on XST) relative to the chosen configuration, meaning AISA's headline numbers depend on threshold tuning on a validation set rather than a parameter-free mechanism. The safety/defense datasets used for probe training (ALL-4) are synthetic/curated by the authors, and the authors themselves state 'we credit the success of AISA half to the construction of the ALL-4 dataset,' which is a training-data dependency rather than a property of the detection method alone. The method is explicitly scoped away from white-box, parameter-modifying attacks as its primary threat model (though claims some residual defense there). All reported baselines and model families are open-source or API models the authors chose; no adaptive attacker that specifically targets the probed attention heads/thresholds is evaluated (i.e., no evaluation against an adversary aware of AISA's detection mechanism, which is a common omission this paper shares with many detector-based defenses). Reported accuracy differences against the strongest API baseline (GPT-5-mini) are within single-digit percentage points, and on individual datasets AISA does not always win (e.g., FQ-PH, WJB show AISA below the commercial detectors in Table 1).

datasets_used: EJ-OO (1.8k, 9 algorithmic jailbreak types + OpenOrca benign)
JBC (1.3k, from a community jailbreak classifier's data)
ALL-4 (aggregation of the above four, ~8k/3k/3k train/val/test)
AEGIS-2 (AEG2, ~33k samples, full train/val/test splits, used for spatial localization and as a comparison training set)
quantitative_results: Detection mean ACC/COR across 13 datasets: AISA[Llama3.1-8b-I]=0.9193, AISA[Llama2-13b-I]=0.9205, AISA[Mistral-7b-I]=0.9014, AISA[Qwen3-8b-I]=0.9126, AISA[GPT-OSS-20b-I]=0.8900
Academic baselines: Jailbreak-Classifier=0.8618, GradSafe=0.8324, NemoGuard-JailbreakDetect=0.7928, SPDetector=0.6444, Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-86M=0.5519
Strong-Reject scores (Table 3, Mistral-7b-U base, lower=safer): vanilla mean ~3.06 (ALL-4=2.664, HB=3.065); AISA mean ~1.05-1.5 (ALL-4=1.535, ADVB=1.008, HB=1.050)

Utility preservation (Table 4): Mistral-7b-U vs AISA[Mistral-7b-U] -- GSM8K 75.51 vs 75.52, BoolQ 86.72 vs 86.72, MMLU 61.90 vs 61.92, MMLU-Pro 36.62 vs 36.62; Llama3.1-8b-I vs AISA -- GSM8K 82.79 vs 82.79, MMLU 71.44 vs 71.44

Threshold ablation (Table 5, Llama3.1-8b): final config SOT=0.4/FRT=0.85/linear gives SR(ALL-4)=1.3000, FRC(XST)=14, FRC(OKT)=62; aggressive SOT=0.15 gives SR(ALL-4)=1.1218 but FRC(XST)=47, FRC(OKT)=95
Signal head footprint: top-K=16 heads, ~0.004M parameters (0.004 MB at 16-bit)
3-sigma cosine-similarity significance threshold for 128-dim head vectors: 0.265 (used to assess cross-model shared representation)
baselines_compared: Detection: GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-5-mini, Llama-Prompt-Guard-2 (22M/86M), NemoGuard-JailbreakDetect, GradSafe, SPDetector, Jailbreak-Classifier, FreeJailbreakDetection (9 total)
Defense: ICD, SAGE, SafeDecoding, SCANS, SelfDefenD (5 total)
Related mechanistic-analysis works discussed (not baselines): SafetyHeadAttribution, AHASI (Arai et al. 2025), Arditi et al. 2024 refusal-direction steering
